text: Hello, I'm sharing a case of perforated diverticulitis. This was the original scan of the patient coming into the emergency room. You see a developing posterior abscess there, maybe something along the iliacs, and some scattered free air. After forty-eight hours of clinical conservative management, the patient continued to spike fevers, so they had another CAT scan, and now you can see these dominant collections. Although the posterior one was drainable by interventional radiology, through a posterior approach, I had a conversation with the patient and offered a laparoscopic drainage of the abscesses for this HINCHY-II diverticulitis.

The surgeon inserts a 5mm trocar in the left upper quadrant, observing no gross contamination in the abdomen and pelvis. Additional trocars are placed in the right lower and right mid quadrants. The surgeon begins maneuvering towards the area behind the uterus to access the first abscess.

The surgeon gently manipulates the epiploic appendages on the sigmoid colon while visualizing the uterus. They attempt to access the lateral abscess cavity, observing pus emerging from the site. The surgeon pauses to take cultures before continuing to work behind the uterus.

The surgeon observes the inflamed rectum behind the uterus, affected by the adjacent abscess cavity. The left ovary is visible, positioned within the cavity. Due to the inflammation, the surgeon decides against an immediate anastomosis, opting instead for lavage and drain placement to facilitate recovery before a future resection.
The surgeon navigates laterally and enters a second abscess cavity, demonstrating the complexity of the case and the need for direct surgical intervention rather than interventional radiology.

The surgeon performs a leak test on the sigmoid colon to identify the perforation site. Upon locating the hole, a stitch is placed, buttressed with pericolic fat or omentum. A drain is positioned in the deep pelvis, with its tip curled back into the cavity along the iliacs.

The surgeon places omentum down into the pelvis to cover the surgical area, ensuring coverage over the site. The case concludes smoothly, with the patient discharged on post-operative day two, retaining a drain for future outpatient management.
